'language': 'ja'} [[JA:大山神社 (西ノ島町)]] {{wikidata link|Q11434677}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oyama Shrine |Image = [[File:Ooyama jinja.JPG|270px]]
Main shrine |Location = {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Nishinoshima Town|Q543193}}Mita 174 |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Oyama Mimikoto}} |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikiuchisha}} (small), old {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}Transformed copper plate roof |alias= |Regular festival = July 13th |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Oyama Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }}'''Oyama Shrine'' (Oyama Jinja) is a {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Nishinoshima Town|Q543193}}. {{jalink|Shikiuchi-sha}} is located at the north foot of {{jalink|Yakizan|Q11567667}}, the highest peak of {{jalink|Dozen|Dozen|Q11476216|Dozen Nishinoshima}}, to the east of Otsu village. It had a close relationship with {{jalink|Yakehi Shrine|Q11567668}}, which was widely worshiped as {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} and was enshrined on the slope of Yakevolcano. === Enshrined God === *{{jalink|Mikoto Oyama}} Although I don't see any particular theories, it is said that the yakivolcano that was originally called ``Oyama'' was {{jalink|Shintai|Q327532}} ({{jalink|Shintaiyama|Q1595289}}), and that the mountain itself was worshiped {{sfn|Edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. This god and {{jalink|Usukega no Mikoto Shrine|Q11449116|Usukega no Mikoto}} visit {{jalink|Hinamajhime no Mikoto Shrine|Q133940165|Hinamajhime no Mikoto}} There is a tradition that Uukega no Mikoto won due to the interference of {{jalink|Amanejaki|Q2417460}}.{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}} After that, {{jalink|Yakikazan|Q11567667}} and {{jalink|Iekatsuyama}} (Atodo-san) competed again over {{jalink|Sakaki}}, and this time it was Heaven. It is said that the demons got in the way of Uukega no Mikoto, resulting in the victory of the god of Oyama, and as a result, sakaki trees flourished on Yakivolcano, but no sakaki trees grew on Mt. Ietaka {{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. It is said that the two gods are not on good terms because of this kind of relationship.{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. === History == =The date of its creation is unknown, but as mentioned above, it is believed that it was originally established as a shrine to enshrine Daisen (a volcanic volcano) as a sacred mountain. Furthermore, 200 meters west of the enshrinement site is the {{jalink|Hyogo Ruins}}, which appears to be a {{jalink|ritual ruins}} from the middle to late {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}}, and it is assumed that water or spring rituals related to agriculture were held there, but its relationship with this shrine is unknown {{sfn|Heibonsha edition|1995}}.It was known in the central government from an early period, and is listed in "{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932}}" as a small shrine in {{jalink|Oki Province|Q1063829}}{{jalink|Chibu District|Q11584565}}. It is not known that the award of |Junior Third Rank |Q11591025}} is known, but in the ``{{jalink|Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11420490|Inshu Shinto Name Book}}'' there is no rank called ``Junior Third Rank Upper(Mama){{efn|Junior Third Rank Upper. }} Daisen Myojin” is written there. In the late {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} (around the 11th - 12th century), when {{jalink|Sea of Japan|Q27092}} water transport in the {{jalink|San'in region|Q398310}} began to develop in earnest, (Japan)||Kami}} came to be revered as the god of safe navigation, and Unjoji Temple (formerly known as Yakehi Shrine) was founded by a {{jalink|ascetic monk}} as a {{jalink|sacred place}} for {{jalink|mountain training}}. {{sfn|Heibonsha Edition|1995}}, and that {{jalink|Bettoto|Q2900274}} came to serve as the shrine's {{jalink|Negi||Negishiki}} {{sfn|Shikiuchisha Research Group|1983}}. (→See also "{{jalink|Yakehi Shrine|Q11567668}}".)In the Middle Ages, it seems that the {{jalink|Izumo Genji|Q11395897|Sasaki clan}}, the {{jalink|Izumo Genji|Q11395897|Sasaki clan}}, who aimed for stable control of Oki Province, tried to take control of this shrine, and {{jalink|Kenji| Q1327993}}On September 5, 2016 ({{jalink|1276|Q5530}}{{jalink|October 13|Q2925}}), the governor of Oki Province, {{jalink|Yasukiyo Sasaki|Q11382304}}, During the Negi era, he issued a letter to Jiren, a monk whose position was in crisis due to a conflict with the shogun, and relieved the Negi position and incorporated it into the ruling party. nk|Bottom|Q11360924}}'' (Kasagi family documents){{efn|Jiren's ancestral office (Oyamasha Negi) and Kanda (Mitasho), which had been confiscated because he had violated the peasants, were returned to Jiren as a gift. }}{{sfn|Inoue|1982}}. {{Quotation|Bottom ({{jalink|Yasukiyo Sasaki|Q11382304}} Kao)
{{jalink|Oki Province|Q1063829|Oki Province}} ({{jalink|Chibu District|Q11584565}}) Mitashō Oyamasha Negi Jobji.
　　　　　　Monk Jiren
On the right, Neyi shokusha, Jirenji Shigedai Sōdensho shōshō.
Farmers who disobeyed orders, etc., 4th dan in Kandauchi, Negishoki,
Great service, great service, etc. Reiwa Yoyuki
The superior, Nyohonsho Reikan Supplementary. Regular/Temporary Go
Shinto rituals, departures, and prayers are carefully conducted.
He was Kanda Hatake, his predecessor, and his deceased father
Master Tsunemoto. Social Affairs Jirenka Coverage, Shigosho
Author: Ibunigita, Kabisatairi, Wusatajin
Farmers and others are aware of this. The situation is unavoidable.
September 5, 2nd year of Kenji | Oki Kasagi document “Sasaki Yasukiyoshita Chisho 'Kamakura Ibun' Vol. 16 No. 12466'
('Kamakura Ibun' Vol. 16 No. 12466)}} The following year, in April of {{jalink|Kenji|Q1327993}}3 (around May of {{jalink|1277|Q5531}}), Yasukiyo became the eighth son, {{jalink|Takaoka Muneyasu|Q6129881 }} was dispatched as the guardian of Oki Province, and a letter was issued with a seal on the sleeve proving that the monk Jiren was a ``Hachiro-den Gotenjin'' and that he was not under the control of the chief official of Mita-sho in Oki Province {{sfn|Inoue|1982}}.{{Quotation|Bottom ({{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}} Kao)
Hachiro-dono, a resident of Mitasho in Oki, a priest of Jirenho {{efn|``Hachiro-dono'' refers to {{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}}. }}
Hitoya Goten. The conviction of the criminal, the representative of the government, etc.
No left or right is allowed. Young person who is troubled at that time, possible
Shinshi Hoso no Jyojo {{efn| (parallel translation) ``Lowered by {{jalink|Takaoka Muneyasu|Q6129881}}, the guardian of Oki Province. Jiren Hoshi, a resident of Mitashō, Chio District, Oki Province (the head of Oyama Shrine whose deity is {{jalink|Yakizan|Q11567667}}) is a retainer of {{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}}. Even if there is a crime, the decision should not be made under the jurisdiction of the shogun (local government official) of Mita-sho. If any troublesome situation arises, please report the details."
April, Kenji 3 It is collected as ``Subun'', and {{harv|Inoue|1982}} attributes this to Yasukiyo's eighth son, {{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}} (Hachiro). There is no dispute that ``Hachiro-dono'' in the text refers to Muneyasu, but because of the question of whether he should call himself ``Hachiro-dono,'' many say that it is a draft of ``{{jalink|Sasaki Yasukiyo|Q11382304}},'' his father and the governor of Oki, but as pointed out by (Inoue 1982), the kaoki belongs to ``Muneyasu'' and is different from Yasukiyo's. This was around the time when Muneyasu had first assumed the position of shugodai, and the document was prepared by someone other than Muneyasu (probably from Jiren's side), and Muneyasu added his personal stamp to the document. It seems that there was a desire to break away from the control of the shogunate, including the parishioners of Oyama Shrine, and judging from the nature of the document, the point made by Inoue 1982 that it was a draft by Muneyasu is correct. }}''
(Kamakura Ibun Vol. 17 No. 12724)}}Furthermore, {{jalink|median (Era name)|Q1327153|Shochu}}3 ({{jalink|1326|Q5782}}), he also held the position of Shugo and was the grandson of Yasukiyo.{{jalink|Munekiyo Sasaki|Q221272 48|A person presumed to be Munekiyo}} ``entrusted the monk Renjo to Kenji's {{jalink|shimochi|Q18226421|shimochi}}'' and assigned him to serve as a Negi-sho in social affairs and below. |Q11626805}} ``Certain Sleeve Handbook'' (Kasagi Family Documents) dated April 7, 1977{{efn|Both Jiren and Renjo have been enshrined as ``shosho handed down from generation to generation'' (jobs handed down from generation to generation). These {{jalink|shrine priests}} are believed to have been {{jalink|betto|Q2900274}} of Unjo-ji Temple {{sfn|edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. }}. On the other hand, the shrine side (Negishiki side) has also been concerned about the current Mita area, which is centered on the enshrinement area, since it was organized into {{jalink|manors|Q1550557}} and became {{jalink|Mita-sho}} (also called {{jalink|Mita-in}}), by directly connecting with the shugo power. It is said that this is where the close relationship between the two arose, with the intention of escaping from the ruling system.{{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}{{efn|In addition, Mita-sho is thought to be a manor established in the eastern half of Nishinoshima, centered around present-day Mita, Nishinoshima-cho, extending from Beppu in the east to Uragato in the west. }}. However, this relationship underwent a major change around {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho period}}, and {{jalink|Kenmu (Japan)|Q1193970|In the first year of Kenmu}} ({{jalink|1334|Q6007}}), the {{jalink|daikan|Q10263740}} of Nishiryo and { Doken of {jalink|Kumon|Q11392042}}, at the behest of his feudal lord, set aside a part of Shonai as ``Oyamamiya Negibun Koushi (malt) rice farm.'' “Koshiryoba” is {{jalink|Divine sake|Q33137 22}} It appears to be {{jalink|hatake}} for supplying {{jalink|koji|Q865501|koji}} for brewing {{sfn|edited by Heibonsha|1995}}. }}, a situation appeared where Mitashō was being incorporated under the control, and in {{jalink|Oan|Q1146245}}2 ({{jalink|1369|Q6211}}) The post of Negi, which became vacant after Renjo's death, was given to Doken Kumon, so ``Certain {{jalink|letter of fulfillment|Q11388108}}'' (Kasagi family document) dated June 24, 2011. , it is assumed that it was completely incorporated into Mitasho, and after this point no documents related to this shrine can be seen, and the fact that related documents from before that have been handed down to the Kasagi clan, who are thought to be descendants of Doken (current Kasagi family documents), is thought to indicate this {{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}.The subsequent history is not made clear, but in the early modern period it seems to have been subsumed into Unjo-ji Temple, which was called "{{jalink|Yakikazan Daigongen}}", and although the Kasagi clan held the {{jalink|Shinto Priest|Q15888735|Shinto Priest}}, Unjo-ji Temple was in charge as a Bettoto. (Unit)|Q855048|stone}} "Oki Kokishu" of 1823, which is said to have been {{jalink|sharyo}}{{jalink|Bunsei|Q1327119}}. is actually {{jalink|Chigyo|Q11584648}} of Unjouji Temple, and it was finally realized that the ``Oyama God'' was {{jalink|Yakebi Gongen}} (Unjouji Temple). {{sfn|Edited by Shikinai Society Research Group|1983}}{{efn|As an example of the view of Oyama no Kami as Yakibi Gongen, {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 7th year (1667) '{ {jalink|Inshu Jōgoki|Q11659057}}'' says, ``Azuruni (Engishiki) Shinto name book, there is Oyama Shrine in Chibu district, this is the god of fire on the mountain (original Chinese)'', and ``{{jalin k|Dainihonshi|Q1156003|Dainipponshiji Jingishi}}, there is a statement that says, ``Oyama Shrine is located on the top of the Yakivolcano mountain in Hashimura, Imamida-go, and is called Yakihi Myojin (original Chinese).'' }}. In the first year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}, {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}}, the shrine separated from Yakibi Gongen, and the official name of the shrine was ``Oyama Shrine'' according to the ``Engishiki'' ceremony, and in the 5th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), it became a village shrine. === Rituals === === Shinto ritual === In "Inshu Fudoki" written in {{jalink|Tenpo|Q1369045}} 4 ({{jalink|1833|Q7587}}), the festival dates are February 13th and June 14th, but after the implementation of {{jalink|solar calendar|Q210953}}, the current regular festival day is July 13th. === Priest === After the Middle Ages, the Betto-shoku of Unjo-ji Temple also served as the Negi-shoku, but in the late period, it is thought that the Kasagi family {{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}, which seems to have taken on the Kumon-shoku of Mitashō, came to attack the Negi-shoku.In the early modern period, when Unjo-ji Temple became widely revered, Although the Kasagi family, who was in charge of the temple and served as {{jalink|Ōshoya}}, called themselves ``Shinto priests'', their actual involvement was limited to {{jalink|Danna|Q11544585}} {{sfn|Edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. When the Meiji era policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism was carried out, in 1888, the betto of Unjo-ji Temple became {{jalink|returning to secular life|Q2583238}} and became the priest (currently {{jalink|guji|Q11453500}}) using the surname Matsuura, and since then the Matsuura clan has inherited the position of chief priest. === Shrine building === {{jalink|Main shrine|Q2044994}} is 1 room long and has a {{jalink|mukōhai|Q11415970}} of {{jalink|gable #gable style|Q4881511|kara gable}} on the front. It is similar to |Q3193781}}, but the facing bow is a Karahafu and its width is narrower than the width of the {{jalink|main building}} roof), and {{jalink|chigi/katsuoki|Q87220234}} is placed on the {{jalink|ridge|ridge|Q800093}}. Rebuilt in 1899. A passage hall with 1 beam and 3 beams extends from the bottom of the worship hall, and continues with the {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} with 5 beams and 4 beams. All have roofs {{jalink|copper plate}}. The {{jalink|torii|Q234731}} located down the stone steps in front of the shrine is {{jalink|granite||made ​​of granite}} from {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}12 ({{jalink|1699|Q7710}}). It is the second oldest stone torii gate on Oki Island. There is also a {{jalink|Shinsensho}} and a Mikoshi storehouse that stores {{jalink|Mikoshi|Q979940}}. === Others === * Kasagi family documents - {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205|Kamakura}} and {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho}} / {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845|A group of ancient documents centered on the early Muromachi period. Contains items related to our shrine and Oyama Shrine. It was designated as a cultural property (ancient document) of Shimane Prefecture on August 12, 1975 ({{jalink|1975|Q2479}}) (part of it was added on March 28, {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}). === Inoue|Q11372970}}|journal=Dozen cultural assets|issue=10|publisher=Oki Dozen Board of Education|year=1980|ref={{sfnref|Inoue|1980}}}} *{{cite journal|Japanese book|title=Medieval Oki no Kumon|author=Hiroshi Inoue|journal=Dozen cultural assets|issue=12|publisher=Oki Dozen Board of Education date=1982-12|ref={{sfnref|Inoue|1982}}}} *{{cite book|Japanese book|title=Shikiuchisha Investigation Report Volume 21 San'in Road 4|editor=Shikiuchisha Research Group|publisher=Kogakukan University Publishing Department|year=1983|ref={{sfnref|Edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}}} *{{cite book|Japanese book|author=Kazumasa Nagami|title=Kurokimura Magazine|publisher=Kurokimura Magazine Editorial Committee|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/ja/pid/3449471|year=1968|ref={{sfnref|Nagami|1968}}}} *{{cite book|Japanese book|title=Place names of Shimane prefecture|series=Japanese historical place name series 33|publisher={{jalink|Heibonsha|Q11482108}}|editor=Heibonsha|year=1995|ISBN=4-582-91017-3|ref={{sfnref|Heibonsha edition|1995}}}} === Related items === * {{jalink|List of Shikinai companies in Oki 'language': 'ja'} [[JA:大山神社 (西ノ島町)]] {{wikidata link|Q11434677}} == Japanese content == {{Shrine |Name = Oyama Shrine |Image = [[File:Ooyama jinja.JPG|270px]]
Main shrine |Location = {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Nishinoshima Town|Q543193}}Mita 174 |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Oyama Mimikoto}} |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikiuchisha}} (small), old {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}Transformed copper plate roof |alias= |Regular festival = July 13th |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Oyama Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }}'''Oyama Shrine'' (Oyama Jinja) is a {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Nishinoshima Town|Q543193}}. {{jalink|Shikiuchi-sha}} is located at the north foot of {{jalink|Yakizan|Q11567667}}, the highest peak of {{jalink|Dozen|Dozen|Q11476216|Dozen Nishinoshima}}, to the east of Otsu village. It had a close relationship with {{jalink|Yakehi Shrine|Q11567668}}, which was widely worshiped as {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} and was enshrined on the slope of Yakevolcano. === Enshrined God === *{{jalink|Mikoto Oyama}} Although I don't see any particular theories, it is said that the yakivolcano that was originally called ``Oyama'' was {{jalink|Shintai|Q327532}} ({{jalink|Shintaiyama|Q1595289}}), and that the mountain itself was worshiped {{sfn|Edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. This god and {{jalink|Usukega no Mikoto Shrine|Q11449116|Usukega no Mikoto}} visit {{jalink|Hinamajhime no Mikoto Shrine|Q133940165|Hinamajhime no Mikoto}} There is a tradition that Uukega no Mikoto won due to the interference of {{jalink|Amanejaki|Q2417460}}.{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}} After that, {{jalink|Yakikazan|Q11567667}} and {{jalink|Iekatsuyama}} (Atodo-san) competed again over {{jalink|Sakaki}}, and this time it was Heaven. It is said that the demons got in the way of Uukega no Mikoto, resulting in the victory of the god of Oyama, and as a result, sakaki trees flourished on Yakivolcano, but no sakaki trees grew on Mt. Ietaka {{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. It is said that the two gods are not on good terms because of this kind of relationship.{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. === History == =The date of its creation is unknown, but as mentioned above, it is believed that it was originally established as a shrine to enshrine Daisen (a volcanic volcano) as a sacred mountain. Furthermore, 200 meters west of the enshrinement site is the {{jalink|Hyogo Ruins}}, which appears to be a {{jalink|ritual ruins}} from the middle to late {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}}, and it is assumed that water or spring rituals related to agriculture were held there, but its relationship with this shrine is unknown {{sfn|Heibonsha edition|1995}}.It was known in the central government from an early period, and is listed in "{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932}}" as a small shrine in {{jalink|Oki Province|Q1063829}}{{jalink|Chibu District|Q11584565}}. It is not known that the award of |Junior Third Rank |Q11591025}} is known, but in the ``{{jalink|Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11420490|Inshu Shinto Name Book}}'' there is no rank called ``Junior Third Rank Upper(Mama){{efn|Junior Third Rank Upper. }} Daisen Myojin” is written there. In the late {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} (around the 11th - 12th century), when {{jalink|Sea of Japan|Q27092}} water transport in the {{jalink|San'in region|Q398310}} began to develop in earnest, (Japan)||Kami}} came to be revered as the god of safe navigation, and Unjoji Temple (formerly known as Yakehi Shrine) was founded by a {{jalink|ascetic monk}} as a {{jalink|sacred place}} for {{jalink|mountain training}}. {{sfn|Heibonsha Edition|1995}}, and that {{jalink|Bettoto|Q2900274}} came to serve as the shrine's {{jalink|Negi||Negishiki}} {{sfn|Shikiuchisha Research Group|1983}}. (→See also "{{jalink|Yakehi Shrine|Q11567668}}".)In the Middle Ages, it seems that the {{jalink|Izumo Genji|Q11395897|Sasaki clan}}, the {{jalink|Izumo Genji|Q11395897|Sasaki clan}}, who aimed for stable control of Oki Province, tried to take control of this shrine, and {{jalink|Kenji| Q1327993}}On September 5, 2016 ({{jalink|1276|Q5530}}{{jalink|October 13|Q2925}}), the governor of Oki Province, {{jalink|Yasukiyo Sasaki|Q11382304}}, During the Negi era, he issued a letter to Jiren, a monk whose position was in crisis due to a conflict with the shogun, and relieved the Negi position and incorporated it into the ruling party. nk|Bottom|Q11360924}}'' (Kasagi family documents){{efn|Jiren's ancestral office (Oyamasha Negi) and Kanda (Mitasho), which had been confiscated because he had violated the peasants, were returned to Jiren as a gift. }}{{sfn|Inoue|1982}}. {{Quotation|Bottom ({{jalink|Yasukiyo Sasaki|Q11382304}} Kao)
{{jalink|Oki Province|Q1063829|Oki Province}} ({{jalink|Chibu District|Q11584565}}) Mitashō Oyamasha Negi Jobji.

Shinto rituals, departures, and prayers are carefully conducted.

Farmers and others are aware of this. The situation is unavoidable.
September 5, 2nd year of Kenji | Oki Kasagi document “Sasaki Yasukiyoshita Chisho 'Kamakura Ibun' Vol. 16 No. 12466'
('Kamakura Ibun' Vol. 16 No. 12466)}} The following year, in April of {{jalink|Kenji|Q1327993}}3 (around May of {{jalink|1277|Q5531}}), Yasukiyo became the eighth son, {{jalink|Takaoka Muneyasu|Q6129881 }} was dispatched as the guardian of Oki Province, and a letter was issued with a seal on the sleeve proving that the monk Jiren was a ``Hachiro-den Gotenjin'' and that he was not under the control of the chief official of Mita-sho in Oki Province {{sfn|Inoue|1982}}.{{Quotation|Bottom ({{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}} Kao)
Hachiro-dono, a resident of Mitasho in Oki, a priest of Jirenho {{efn|``Hachiro-dono'' refers to {{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}}. }}
Hitoya Goten. The conviction of the criminal, the representative of the government, etc.
No left or right is allowed. Young person who is troubled at that time, possible
Shinshi Hoso no Jyojo {{efn| (parallel translation) ``Lowered by {{jalink|Takaoka Muneyasu|Q6129881}}, the guardian of Oki Province. Jiren Hoshi, a resident of Mitashō, Chio District, Oki Province (the head of Oyama Shrine whose deity is {{jalink|Yakizan|Q11567667}}) is a retainer of {{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}}. Even if there is a crime, the decision should not be made under the jurisdiction of the shogun (local government official) of Mita-sho. If any troublesome situation arises, please report the details."
April, Kenji 3 It is collected as ``Subun'', and {{harv|Inoue|1982}} attributes this to Yasukiyo's eighth son, {{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}} (Hachiro). There is no dispute that ``Hachiro-dono'' in the text refers to Muneyasu, but because of the question of whether he should call himself ``Hachiro-dono,'' many say that it is a draft of ``{{jalink|Sasaki Yasukiyo|Q11382304}},'' his father and the governor of Oki, but as pointed out by (Inoue 1982), the kaoki belongs to ``Muneyasu'' and is different from Yasukiyo's. This was around the time when Muneyasu had first assumed the position of shugodai, and the document was prepared by someone other than Muneyasu (probably from Jiren's side), and Muneyasu added his personal stamp to the document. It seems that there was a desire to break away from the control of the shogunate, including the parishioners of Oyama Shrine, and judging from the nature of the document, the point made by Inoue 1982 that it was a draft by Muneyasu is correct. }}''
(Kamakura Ibun Vol. 17 No. 12724)}}Furthermore, {{jalink|median (Era name)|Q1327153|Shochu}}3 ({{jalink|1326|Q5782}}), he also held the position of Shugo and was the grandson of Yasukiyo.{{jalink|Munekiyo Sasaki|Q221272 48|A person presumed to be Munekiyo}} ``entrusted the monk Renjo to Kenji's {{jalink|shimochi|Q18226421|shimochi}}'' and assigned him to serve as a Negi-sho in social affairs and below. |Q11626805}} ``Certain Sleeve Handbook'' (Kasagi Family Documents) dated April 7, 1977{{efn|Both Jiren and Renjo have been enshrined as ``shosho handed down from generation to generation'' (jobs handed down from generation to generation). These {{jalink|shrine priests}} are believed to have been {{jalink|betto|Q2900274}} of Unjo-ji Temple {{sfn|edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. }}. On the other hand, the shrine side (Negishiki side) has also been concerned about the current Mita area, which is centered on the enshrinement area, since it was organized into {{jalink|manors|Q1550557}} and became {{jalink|Mita-sho}} (also called {{jalink|Mita-in}}), by directly connecting with the shugo power. It is said that this is where the close relationship between the two arose, with the intention of escaping from the ruling system.{{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}{{efn|In addition, Mita-sho is thought to be a manor established in the eastern half of Nishinoshima, centered around present-day Mita, Nishinoshima-cho, extending from Beppu in the east to Uragato in the west. }}. However, this relationship underwent a major change around {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho period}}, and {{jalink|Kenmu (Japan)|Q1193970|In the first year of Kenmu}} ({{jalink|1334|Q6007}}), the {{jalink|daikan|Q10263740}} of Nishiryo and { Doken of {jalink|Kumon|Q11392042}}, at the behest of his feudal lord, set aside a part of Shonai as ``Oyamamiya Negibun Koushi (malt) rice farm.'' “Koshiryoba” is {{jalink|Divine sake|Q33137 22}} It appears to be {{jalink|hatake}} for supplying {{jalink|koji|Q865501|koji}} for brewing {{sfn|edited by Heibonsha|1995}}. }}, a situation appeared where Mitashō was being incorporated under the control, and in {{jalink|Oan|Q1146245}}2 ({{jalink|1369|Q6211}}) The post of Negi, which became vacant after Renjo's death, was given to Doken Kumon, so ``Certain {{jalink|letter of fulfillment|Q11388108}}'' (Kasagi family document) dated June 24, 2011. , it is assumed that it was completely incorporated into Mitasho, and after this point no documents related to this shrine can be seen, and the fact that related documents from before that have been handed down to the Kasagi clan, who are thought to be descendants of Doken (current Kasagi family documents), is thought to indicate this {{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}.The subsequent history is not made clear, but in the early modern period it seems to have been subsumed into Unjo-ji Temple, which was called "{{jalink|Yakikazan Daigongen}}", and although the Kasagi clan held the {{jalink|Shinto Priest|Q15888735|Shinto Priest}}, Unjo-ji Temple was in charge as a Bettoto. (Unit)|Q855048|stone}} "Oki Kokishu" of 1823, which is said to have been {{jalink|sharyo}}{{jalink|Bunsei|Q1327119}}. is actually {{jalink|Chigyo|Q11584648}} of Unjouji Temple, and it was finally realized that the ``Oyama God'' was {{jalink|Yakebi Gongen}} (Unjouji Temple). {{sfn|Edited by Shikinai Society Research Group|1983}}{{efn|As an example of the view of Oyama no Kami as Yakibi Gongen, {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 7th year (1667) '{ {jalink|Inshu Jōgoki|Q11659057}}'' says, ``Azuruni (Engishiki) Shinto name book, there is Oyama Shrine in Chibu district, this is the god of fire on the mountain (original Chinese)'', and ``{{jalin k|Dainihonshi|Q1156003|Dainipponshiji Jingishi}}, there is a statement that says, ``Oyama Shrine is located on the top of the Yakivolcano mountain in Hashimura, Imamida-go, and is called Yakihi Myojin (original Chinese).'' }}. In the first year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}, {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}}, the shrine separated from Yakibi Gongen, and the official name of the shrine was ``Oyama Shrine'' according to the ``Engishiki'' ceremony, and in the 5th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), it became a village shrine. === Rituals === === Shinto ritual === In "Inshu Fudoki" written in {{jalink|Tenpo|Q1369045}} 4 ({{jalink|1833|Q7587}}), the festival dates are February 13th and June 14th, but after the implementation of {{jalink|solar calendar|Q210953}}, the current regular festival day is July 13th. === Priest === After the Middle Ages, the Betto-shoku of Unjo-ji Temple also served as the Negi-shoku, but in the late period, it is thought that the Kasagi family {{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}, which seems to have taken on the Kumon-shoku of Mitashō, came to attack the Negi-shoku.In the early modern period, when Unjo-ji Temple became widely revered, Although the Kasagi family, who was in charge of the temple and served as {{jalink|Ōshoya}}, called themselves ``Shinto priests'', their actual involvement was limited to {{jalink|Danna|Q11544585}} {{sfn|Edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. When the Meiji era policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism was carried out, in 1888, the betto of Unjo-ji Temple became {{jalink|returning to secular life|Q2583238}} and became the priest (currently {{jalink|guji|Q11453500}}) using the surname Matsuura, and since then the Matsuura clan has inherited the position of chief priest. === Shrine building === {{jalink|Main shrine|Q2044994}} is 1 room long and has a {{jalink|mukōhai|Q11415970}} of {{jalink|gable #gable style|Q4881511|kara gable}} on the front. It is similar to |Q3193781}}, but the facing bow is a Karahafu and its width is narrower than the width of the {{jalink|main building}} roof), and {{jalink|chigi/katsuoki|Q87220234}} is placed on the {{jalink|ridge|ridge|Q800093}}. Rebuilt in 1899. A passage hall with 1 beam and 3 beams extends from the bottom of the worship hall, and continues with the {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} with 5 beams and 4 beams. All have roofs {{jalink|copper plate}}. The {{jalink|torii|Q234731}} located down the stone steps in front of the shrine is {{jalink|granite||made ​​of granite}} from {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}12 ({{jalink|1699|Q7710}}). It is the second oldest stone torii gate on Oki Island. There is also a {{jalink|Shinsensho}} and a Mikoshi storehouse that stores {{jalink|Mikoshi|Q979940}}. === Others === * Kasagi family documents - {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205|Kamakura}} and {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho}} / {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845|A group of ancient documents centered on the early Muromachi period. Contains items related to our shrine and Oyama Shrine. It was designated as a cultural property (ancient document) of Shimane Prefecture on August 12, 1975 ({{jalink|1975|Q2479}}) (part of it was added on March 28, {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}). === Main shrine |Location = {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Nishinoshima Town|Q543193}}Mita 174 |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Oyama Mimikoto}} |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikiuchisha}} (small), old {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}Transformed copper plate roof |alias= |Regular festival = July 13th |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Oyama Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }}'''Oyama Shrine'' (Oyama Jinja) is a {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Nishinoshima Town|Q543193}}. {{jalink|Shikiuchi-sha}} is located at the north foot of {{jalink|Yakizan|Q11567667}}, the highest peak of {{jalink|Dozen|Dozen|Q11476216|Dozen Nishinoshima}}, to the east of Otsu village. It had a close relationship with {{jalink|Yakehi Shrine|Q11567668}}, which was widely worshiped as {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} and was enshrined on the slope of Yakevolcano. === Enshrined God === *{{jalink|Mikoto Oyama}} Although I don't see any particular theories, it is said that the yakivolcano that was originally called ``Oyama'' was {{jalink|Shintai|Q327532}} ({{jalink|Shintaiyama|Q1595289}}), and that the mountain itself was worshiped {{sfn|Edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. This god and {{jalink|Usukega no Mikoto Shrine|Q11449116|Usukega no Mikoto}} visit {{jalink|Hinamajhime no Mikoto Shrine|Q133940165|Hinamajhime no Mikoto}} There is a tradition that Uukega no Mikoto won due to the interference of {{jalink|Amanejaki|Q2417460}}.{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}} After that, {{jalink|Yakikazan|Q11567667}} and {{jalink|Iekatsuyama}} (Atodo-san) competed again over {{jalink|Sakaki}}, and this time it was Heaven. It is said that the demons got in the way of Uukega no Mikoto, resulting in the victory of the god of Oyama, and as a result, sakaki trees flourished on Yakivolcano, but no sakaki trees grew on Mt. Ietaka {{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. It is said that the two gods are not on good terms because of this kind of relationship.{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. === History == =The date of its creation is unknown, but as mentioned above, it is believed that it was originally established as a shrine to enshrine Daisen (a volcanic volcano) as a sacred mountain. Furthermore, 200 meters west of the enshrinement site is the {{jalink|Hyogo Ruins}}, which appears to be a {{jalink|ritual ruins}} from the middle to late {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}}, and it is assumed that water or spring rituals related to agriculture were held there, but its relationship with this shrine is unknown {{sfn|Heibonsha edition|1995}}.It was known in the central government from an early period, and is listed in "{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932}}" as a small shrine in {{jalink|Oki Province|Q1063829}}{{jalink|Chibu District|Q11584565}}. It is not known that the award of |Junior Third Rank |Q11591025}} is known, but in the ``{{jalink|Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11420490|Inshu Shinto Name Book}}'' there is no rank called ``Junior Third Rank Upper(Mama){{efn|Junior Third Rank Upper. }} Daisen Myojin” is written there. In the late {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} (around the 11th - 12th century), when {{jalink|Sea of Japan|Q27092}} water transport in the {{jalink|San'in region|Q398310}} began to develop in earnest, (Japan)||Kami}} came to be revered as the god of safe navigation, and Unjoji Temple (formerly known as Yakehi Shrine) was founded by a {{jalink|ascetic monk}} as a {{jalink|sacred place}} for {{jalink|mountain training}}. {{sfn|Heibonsha Edition|1995}}, and that {{jalink|Bettoto|Q2900274}} came to serve as the shrine's {{jalink|Negi||Negishiki}} {{sfn|Shikiuchisha Research Group|1983}}. (→See also "{{jalink|Yakehi Shrine|Q11567668}}".)In the Middle Ages, it seems that the {{jalink|Izumo Genji|Q11395897|Sasaki clan}}, the {{jalink|Izumo Genji|Q11395897|Sasaki clan}}, who aimed for stable control of Oki Province, tried to take control of this shrine, and {{jalink|Kenji| Q1327993}}On September 5, 2016 ({{jalink|1276|Q5530}}{{jalink|October 13|Q2925}}), the governor of Oki Province, {{jalink|Yasukiyo Sasaki|Q11382304}}, During the Negi era, he issued a letter to Jiren, a monk whose position was in crisis due to a conflict with the shogun, and relieved the Negi position and incorporated it into the ruling party. nk|Bottom|Q11360924}}'' (Kasagi family documents){{efn|Jiren's ancestral office (Oyamasha Negi) and Kanda (Mitasho), which had been confiscated because he had violated the peasants, were returned to Jiren as a gift. }}{{sfn|Inoue|1982}}. {{Quotation|Bottom ({{jalink|Yasukiyo Sasaki|Q11382304}} Kao)

('Kamakura Ibun' Vol. 16 No. 12466)}} The following year, in April of {{jalink|Kenji|Q1327993}}3 (around May of {{jalink|1277|Q5531}}), Yasukiyo became the eighth son, {{jalink|Takaoka Muneyasu|Q6129881 }} was dispatched as the guardian of Oki Province, and a letter was issued with a seal on the sleeve proving that the monk Jiren was a ``Hachiro-den Gotenjin'' and that he was not under the control of the chief official of Mita-sho in Oki Province {{sfn|Inoue|1982}}.{{Quotation|Bottom ({{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}} Kao)

Shinshi Hoso no Jyojo {{efn| (parallel translation) ``Lowered by {{jalink|Takaoka Muneyasu|Q6129881}}, the guardian of Oki Province. Jiren Hoshi, a resident of Mitashō, Chio District, Oki Province (the head of Oyama Shrine whose deity is {{jalink|Yakizan|Q11567667}}) is a retainer of {{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}}. Even if there is a crime, the decision should not be made under the jurisdiction of the shogun (local government official) of Mita-sho. If any troublesome situation arises, please report the details."
April, Kenji 3 It is collected as ``Subun'', and {{harv|Inoue|1982}} attributes this to Yasukiyo's eighth son, {{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}} (Hachiro). There is no dispute that ``Hachiro-dono'' in the text refers to Muneyasu, but because of the question of whether he should call himself ``Hachiro-dono,'' many say that it is a draft of ``{{jalink|Sasaki Yasukiyo|Q11382304}},'' his father and the governor of Oki, but as pointed out by (Inoue 1982), the kaoki belongs to ``Muneyasu'' and is different from Yasukiyo's. This was around the time when Muneyasu had first assumed the position of shugodai, and the document was prepared by someone other than Muneyasu (probably from Jiren's side), and Muneyasu added his personal stamp to the document. It seems that there was a desire to break away from the control of the shogunate, including the parishioners of Oyama Shrine, and judging from the nature of the document, the point made by Inoue 1982 that it was a draft by Muneyasu is correct. }}''
(Kamakura Ibun Vol. 17 No. 12724)}}Furthermore, {{jalink|median (Era name)|Q1327153|Shochu}}3 ({{jalink|1326|Q5782}}), he also held the position of Shugo and was the grandson of Yasukiyo.{{jalink|Munekiyo Sasaki|Q221272 48|A person presumed to be Munekiyo}} ``entrusted the monk Renjo to Kenji's {{jalink|shimochi|Q18226421|shimochi}}'' and assigned him to serve as a Negi-sho in social affairs and below. |Q11626805}} ``Certain Sleeve Handbook'' (Kasagi Family Documents) dated April 7, 1977{{efn|Both Jiren and Renjo have been enshrined as ``shosho handed down from generation to generation'' (jobs handed down from generation to generation). These {{jalink|shrine priests}} are believed to have been {{jalink|betto|Q2900274}} of Unjo-ji Temple {{sfn|edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. }}. On the other hand, the shrine side (Negishiki side) has also been concerned about the current Mita area, which is centered on the enshrinement area, since it was organized into {{jalink|manors|Q1550557}} and became {{jalink|Mita-sho}} (also called {{jalink|Mita-in}}), by directly connecting with the shugo power. It is said that this is where the close relationship between the two arose, with the intention of escaping from the ruling system.{{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}{{efn|In addition, Mita-sho is thought to be a manor established in the eastern half of Nishinoshima, centered around present-day Mita, Nishinoshima-cho, extending from Beppu in the east to Uragato in the west. }}. However, this relationship underwent a major change around {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho period}}, and {{jalink|Kenmu (Japan)|Q1193970|In the first year of Kenmu}} ({{jalink|1334|Q6007}}), the {{jalink|daikan|Q10263740}} of Nishiryo and { Doken of {jalink|Kumon|Q11392042}}, at the behest of his feudal lord, set aside a part of Shonai as ``Oyamamiya Negibun Koushi (malt) rice farm.'' “Koshiryoba” is {{jalink|Divine sake|Q33137 22}} It appears to be {{jalink|hatake}} for supplying {{jalink|koji|Q865501|koji}} for brewing {{sfn|edited by Heibonsha|1995}}. }}, a situation appeared where Mitashō was being incorporated under the control, and in {{jalink|Oan|Q1146245}}2 ({{jalink|1369|Q6211}}) The post of Negi, which became vacant after Renjo's death, was given to Doken Kumon, so ``Certain {{jalink|letter of fulfillment|Q11388108}}'' (Kasagi family document) dated June 24, 2011. , it is assumed that it was completely incorporated into Mitasho, and after this point no documents related to this shrine can be seen, and the fact that related documents from before that have been handed down to the Kasagi clan, who are thought to be descendants of Doken (current Kasagi family documents), is thought to indicate this {{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}.The subsequent history is not made clear, but in the early modern period it seems to have been subsumed into Unjo-ji Temple, which was called "{{jalink|Yakikazan Daigongen}}", and although the Kasagi clan held the {{jalink|Shinto Priest|Q15888735|Shinto Priest}}, Unjo-ji Temple was in charge as a Bettoto. (Unit)|Q855048|stone}} "Oki Kokishu" of 1823, which is said to have been {{jalink|sharyo}}{{jalink|Bunsei|Q1327119}}. is actually {{jalink|Chigyo|Q11584648}} of Unjouji Temple, and it was finally realized that the ``Oyama God'' was {{jalink|Yakebi Gongen}} (Unjouji Temple). {{sfn|Edited by Shikinai Society Research Group|1983}}{{efn|As an example of the view of Oyama no Kami as Yakibi Gongen, {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 7th year (1667) '{ {jalink|Inshu Jōgoki|Q11659057}}'' says, ``Azuruni (Engishiki) Shinto name book, there is Oyama Shrine in Chibu district, this is the god of fire on the mountain (original Chinese)'', and ``{{jalin k|Dainihonshi|Q1156003|Dainipponshiji Jingishi}}, there is a statement that says, ``Oyama Shrine is located on the top of the Yakivolcano mountain in Hashimura, Imamida-go, and is called Yakihi Myojin (original Chinese).'' }}. In the first year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}, {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}}, the shrine separated from Yakibi Gongen, and the official name of the shrine was ``Oyama Shrine'' according to the ``Engishiki'' ceremony, and in the 5th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), it became a village shrine. === Rituals === === Shinto ritual === In "Inshu Fudoki" written in {{jalink|Tenpo|Q1369045}} 4 ({{jalink|1833|Q7587}}), the festival dates are February 13th and June 14th, but after the implementation of {{jalink|solar calendar|Q210953}}, the current regular festival day is July 13th. === Priest === After the Middle Ages, the Betto-shoku of Unjo-ji Temple also served as the Negi-shoku, but in the late period, it is thought that the Kasagi family {{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}, which seems to have taken on the Kumon-shoku of Mitashō, came to attack the Negi-shoku.In the early modern period, when Unjo-ji Temple became widely revered, Although the Kasagi family, who was in charge of the temple and served as {{jalink|Ōshoya}}, called themselves ``Shinto priests'', their actual involvement was limited to {{jalink|Danna|Q11544585}} {{sfn|Edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. When the Meiji era policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism was carried out, in 1888, the betto of Unjo-ji Temple became {{jalink|returning to secular life|Q2583238}} and became the priest (currently {{jalink|guji|Q11453500}}) using the surname Matsuura, and since then the Matsuura clan has inherited the position of chief priest. === Shrine building === {{jalink|Main shrine|Q2044994}} is 1 room long and has a {{jalink|mukōhai|Q11415970}} of {{jalink|gable #gable style|Q4881511|kara gable}} on the front. It is similar to |Q3193781}}, but the facing bow is a Karahafu and its width is narrower than the width of the {{jalink|main building}} roof), and {{jalink|chigi/katsuoki|Q87220234}} is placed on the {{jalink|ridge|ridge|Q800093}}. Rebuilt in 1899. A passage hall with 1 beam and 3 beams extends from the bottom of the worship hall, and continues with the {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} with 5 beams and 4 beams. All have roofs {{jalink|copper plate}}. The {{jalink|torii|Q234731}} located down the stone steps in front of the shrine is {{jalink|granite||made ​​of granite}} from {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}12 ({{jalink|1699|Q7710}}). It is the second oldest stone torii gate on Oki Island. There is also a {{jalink|Shinsensho}} and a Mikoshi storehouse that stores {{jalink|Mikoshi|Q979940}}. === Others === * Kasagi family documents - {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205|Kamakura}} and {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho}} / {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845|A group of ancient documents centered on the early Muromachi period. Contains items related to our shrine and Oyama Shrine. It was designated as a cultural property (ancient document) of Shimane Prefecture on August 12, 1975 ({{jalink|1975|Q2479}}) (part of it was added on March 28, {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}). === Main shrine |Location = {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Nishinoshima Town|Q543193}}Mita 174 |Position = {{WikidataCoord|display=inline,title}} |Enshrined deity = {{jalink|Oyama Mimikoto}} |Company rank = {{jalink|Shikiuchisha}} (small), old {{jalink|murasha|Q134917284}} |Founded = unknown |Main shrine = {{jalink|Kasuga-zukuri|Q3193781}}Transformed copper plate roof |alias= |Regular festival = July 13th |Shinto ritual = |Map = {{Location map|Japan Shimane Prefecture|label=Oyama Shrine|position=left|width=200|mark=Shinto torii icon vermillion.svg|marksize=15|float=center|caption=}} {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=10|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|frame-height=250}} }}'''Oyama Shrine'' (Oyama Jinja) is a {{jalink|Shrine|Q845945}} located in {{jalink|Shimane Prefecture|Q132751}}{{jalink|Oki District|Q735735}}{{jalink|Nishinoshima Town|Q543193}}. {{jalink|Shikiuchi-sha}} is located at the north foot of {{jalink|Yakizan|Q11567667}}, the highest peak of {{jalink|Dozen|Dozen|Q11476216|Dozen Nishinoshima}}, to the east of Otsu village. It had a close relationship with {{jalink|Yakehi Shrine|Q11567668}}, which was widely worshiped as {{jalink|guardian deity|Q131278957}} and was enshrined on the slope of Yakevolcano. === Enshrined God === *{{jalink|Mikoto Oyama}} Although I don't see any particular theories, it is said that the yakivolcano that was originally called ``Oyama'' was {{jalink|Shintai|Q327532}} ({{jalink|Shintaiyama|Q1595289}}), and that the mountain itself was worshiped {{sfn|Edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. This god and {{jalink|Usukega no Mikoto Shrine|Q11449116|Usukega no Mikoto}} visit {{jalink|Hinamajhime no Mikoto Shrine|Q133940165|Hinamajhime no Mikoto}} There is a tradition that Uukega no Mikoto won due to the interference of {{jalink|Amanejaki|Q2417460}}.{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}} After that, {{jalink|Yakikazan|Q11567667}} and {{jalink|Iekatsuyama}} (Atodo-san) competed again over {{jalink|Sakaki}}, and this time it was Heaven. It is said that the demons got in the way of Uukega no Mikoto, resulting in the victory of the god of Oyama, and as a result, sakaki trees flourished on Yakivolcano, but no sakaki trees grew on Mt. Ietaka {{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. It is said that the two gods are not on good terms because of this kind of relationship.{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. === History == =The date of its creation is unknown, but as mentioned above, it is believed that it was originally established as a shrine to enshrine Daisen (a volcanic volcano) as a sacred mountain. Furthermore, 200 meters west of the enshrinement site is the {{jalink|Hyogo Ruins}}, which appears to be a {{jalink|ritual ruins}} from the middle to late {{jalink|Kofun period|Q459939}}, and it is assumed that water or spring rituals related to agriculture were held there, but its relationship with this shrine is unknown {{sfn|Heibonsha edition|1995}}.It was known in the central government from an early period, and is listed in "{{jalink|Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932}}" as a small shrine in {{jalink|Oki Province|Q1063829}}{{jalink|Chibu District|Q11584565}}. It is not known that the award of |Junior Third Rank |Q11591025}} is known, but in the ``{{jalink|Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11420490|Inshu Shinto Name Book}}'' there is no rank called ``Junior Third Rank Upper(Mama){{efn|Junior Third Rank Upper. }} Daisen Myojin” is written there. In the late {{jalink|Heian period|Q193292}} (around the 11th - 12th century), when {{jalink|Sea of Japan|Q27092}} water transport in the {{jalink|San'in region|Q398310}} began to develop in earnest, (Japan)||Kami}} came to be revered as the god of safe navigation, and Unjoji Temple (formerly known as Yakehi Shrine) was founded by a {{jalink|ascetic monk}} as a {{jalink|sacred place}} for {{jalink|mountain training}}. {{sfn|Heibonsha Edition|1995}}, and that {{jalink|Bettoto|Q2900274}} came to serve as the shrine's {{jalink|Negi||Negishiki}} {{sfn|Shikiuchisha Research Group|1983}}. (→See also "{{jalink|Yakehi Shrine|Q11567668}}".)In the Middle Ages, it seems that the {{jalink|Izumo Genji|Q11395897|Sasaki clan}}, the {{jalink|Izumo Genji|Q11395897|Sasaki clan}}, who aimed for stable control of Oki Province, tried to take control of this shrine, and {{jalink|Kenji| Q1327993}}On September 5, 2016 ({{jalink|1276|Q5530}}{{jalink|October 13|Q2925}}), the governor of Oki Province, {{jalink|Yasukiyo Sasaki|Q11382304}}, During the Negi era, he issued a letter to Jiren, a monk whose position was in crisis due to a conflict with the shogun, and relieved the Negi position and incorporated it into the ruling party. nk|Bottom|Q11360924}}'' (Kasagi family documents){{efn|Jiren's ancestral office (Oyamasha Negi) and Kanda (Mitasho), which had been confiscated because he had violated the peasants, were returned to Jiren as a gift. }}{{sfn|Inoue|1982}}. {{Quotation|Bottom ({{jalink|Yasukiyo Sasaki|Q11382304}} Kao)

('Kamakura Ibun' Vol. 16 No. 12466)}} The following year, in April of {{jalink|Kenji|Q1327993}}3 (around May of {{jalink|1277|Q5531}}), Yasukiyo became the eighth son, {{jalink|Takaoka Muneyasu|Q6129881 }} was dispatched as the guardian of Oki Province, and a letter was issued with a seal on the sleeve proving that the monk Jiren was a ``Hachiro-den Gotenjin'' and that he was not under the control of the chief official of Mita-sho in Oki Province {{sfn|Inoue|1982}}.{{Quotation|Bottom ({{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}} Kao)

Hitoya Goten. The conviction of the criminal, the representative of the government, etc.
No left or right is allowed. Young person who is troubled at that time, possible
Shinshi Hoso no Jyojo {{efn| (parallel translation) ``Lowered by {{jalink|Takaoka Muneyasu|Q6129881}}, the guardian of Oki Province. Jiren Hoshi, a resident of Mitashō, Chio District, Oki Province (the head of Oyama Shrine whose deity is {{jalink|Yakizan|Q11567667}}) is a retainer of {{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}}. Even if there is a crime, the decision should not be made under the jurisdiction of the shogun (local government official) of Mita-sho. If any troublesome situation arises, please report the details."
April, Kenji 3 It is collected as ``Subun'', and {{harv|Inoue|1982}} attributes this to Yasukiyo's eighth son, {{jalink|Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881}} (Hachiro). There is no dispute that ``Hachiro-dono'' in the text refers to Muneyasu, but because of the question of whether he should call himself ``Hachiro-dono,'' many say that it is a draft of ``{{jalink|Sasaki Yasukiyo|Q11382304}},'' his father and the governor of Oki, but as pointed out by (Inoue 1982), the kaoki belongs to ``Muneyasu'' and is different from Yasukiyo's. This was around the time when Muneyasu had first assumed the position of shugodai, and the document was prepared by someone other than Muneyasu (probably from Jiren's side), and Muneyasu added his personal stamp to the document. It seems that there was a desire to break away from the control of the shogunate, including the parishioners of Oyama Shrine, and judging from the nature of the document, the point made by Inoue 1982 that it was a draft by Muneyasu is correct. }}''
(Kamakura Ibun Vol. 17 No. 12724)}}Furthermore, {{jalink|median (Era name)|Q1327153|Shochu}}3 ({{jalink|1326|Q5782}}), he also held the position of Shugo and was the grandson of Yasukiyo.{{jalink|Munekiyo Sasaki|Q221272 48|A person presumed to be Munekiyo}} ``entrusted the monk Renjo to Kenji's {{jalink|shimochi|Q18226421|shimochi}}'' and assigned him to serve as a Negi-sho in social affairs and below. |Q11626805}} ``Certain Sleeve Handbook'' (Kasagi Family Documents) dated April 7, 1977{{efn|Both Jiren and Renjo have been enshrined as ``shosho handed down from generation to generation'' (jobs handed down from generation to generation). These {{jalink|shrine priests}} are believed to have been {{jalink|betto|Q2900274}} of Unjo-ji Temple {{sfn|edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. }}. On the other hand, the shrine side (Negishiki side) has also been concerned about the current Mita area, which is centered on the enshrinement area, since it was organized into {{jalink|manors|Q1550557}} and became {{jalink|Mita-sho}} (also called {{jalink|Mita-in}}), by directly connecting with the shugo power. It is said that this is where the close relationship between the two arose, with the intention of escaping from the ruling system.{{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}{{efn|In addition, Mita-sho is thought to be a manor established in the eastern half of Nishinoshima, centered around present-day Mita, Nishinoshima-cho, extending from Beppu in the east to Uragato in the west. }}. However, this relationship underwent a major change around {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho period}}, and {{jalink|Kenmu (Japan)|Q1193970|In the first year of Kenmu}} ({{jalink|1334|Q6007}}), the {{jalink|daikan|Q10263740}} of Nishiryo and { Doken of {jalink|Kumon|Q11392042}}, at the behest of his feudal lord, set aside a part of Shonai as ``Oyamamiya Negibun Koushi (malt) rice farm.'' “Koshiryoba” is {{jalink|Divine sake|Q33137 22}} It appears to be {{jalink|hatake}} for supplying {{jalink|koji|Q865501|koji}} for brewing {{sfn|edited by Heibonsha|1995}}. }}, a situation appeared where Mitashō was being incorporated under the control, and in {{jalink|Oan|Q1146245}}2 ({{jalink|1369|Q6211}}) The post of Negi, which became vacant after Renjo's death, was given to Doken Kumon, so ``Certain {{jalink|letter of fulfillment|Q11388108}}'' (Kasagi family document) dated June 24, 2011. , it is assumed that it was completely incorporated into Mitasho, and after this point no documents related to this shrine can be seen, and the fact that related documents from before that have been handed down to the Kasagi clan, who are thought to be descendants of Doken (current Kasagi family documents), is thought to indicate this {{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}.The subsequent history is not made clear, but in the early modern period it seems to have been subsumed into Unjo-ji Temple, which was called "{{jalink|Yakikazan Daigongen}}", and although the Kasagi clan held the {{jalink|Shinto Priest|Q15888735|Shinto Priest}}, Unjo-ji Temple was in charge as a Bettoto. (Unit)|Q855048|stone}} "Oki Kokishu" of 1823, which is said to have been {{jalink|sharyo}}{{jalink|Bunsei|Q1327119}}. is actually {{jalink|Chigyo|Q11584648}} of Unjouji Temple, and it was finally realized that the ``Oyama God'' was {{jalink|Yakebi Gongen}} (Unjouji Temple). {{sfn|Edited by Shikinai Society Research Group|1983}}{{efn|As an example of the view of Oyama no Kami as Yakibi Gongen, {{jalink|Kanbun|Q1146228}} 7th year (1667) '{ {jalink|Inshu Jōgoki|Q11659057}}'' says, ``Azuruni (Engishiki) Shinto name book, there is Oyama Shrine in Chibu district, this is the god of fire on the mountain (original Chinese)'', and ``{{jalin k|Dainihonshi|Q1156003|Dainipponshiji Jingishi}}, there is a statement that says, ``Oyama Shrine is located on the top of the Yakivolcano mountain in Hashimura, Imamida-go, and is called Yakihi Myojin (original Chinese).'' }}. In the first year of {{jalink|Meiji|Q3125096}}, {{jalink|Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535}}, the shrine separated from Yakibi Gongen, and the official name of the shrine was ``Oyama Shrine'' according to the ``Engishiki'' ceremony, and in the 5th year of the Meiji era ({{jalink|1872|Q7753}}), it became a village shrine. === Rituals === === Shinto ritual === In "Inshu Fudoki" written in {{jalink|Tenpo|Q1369045}} 4 ({{jalink|1833|Q7587}}), the festival dates are February 13th and June 14th, but after the implementation of {{jalink|solar calendar|Q210953}}, the current regular festival day is July 13th. === Priest === After the Middle Ages, the Betto-shoku of Unjo-ji Temple also served as the Negi-shoku, but in the late period, it is thought that the Kasagi family {{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}, which seems to have taken on the Kumon-shoku of Mitashō, came to attack the Negi-shoku.In the early modern period, when Unjo-ji Temple became widely revered, Although the Kasagi family, who was in charge of the temple and served as {{jalink|Ōshoya}}, called themselves ``Shinto priests'', their actual involvement was limited to {{jalink|Danna|Q11544585}} {{sfn|Edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. When the Meiji era policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism was carried out, in 1888, the betto of Unjo-ji Temple became {{jalink|returning to secular life|Q2583238}} and became the priest (currently {{jalink|guji|Q11453500}}) using the surname Matsuura, and since then the Matsuura clan has inherited the position of chief priest. === Shrine building === {{jalink|Main shrine|Q2044994}} is 1 room long and has a {{jalink|mukōhai|Q11415970}} of {{jalink|gable #gable style|Q4881511|kara gable}} on the front. It is similar to |Q3193781}}, but the facing bow is a Karahafu and its width is narrower than the width of the {{jalink|main building}} roof), and {{jalink|chigi/katsuoki|Q87220234}} is placed on the {{jalink|ridge|ridge|Q800093}}. Rebuilt in 1899. A passage hall with 1 beam and 3 beams extends from the bottom of the worship hall, and continues with the {{jalink|haiden|Q3125745}} with 5 beams and 4 beams. All have roofs {{jalink|copper plate}}. The {{jalink|torii|Q234731}} located down the stone steps in front of the shrine is {{jalink|granite||made ​​of granite}} from {{jalink|Genroku|Q1325001}}12 ({{jalink|1699|Q7710}}). It is the second oldest stone torii gate on Oki Island. There is also a {{jalink|Shinsensho}} and a Mikoshi storehouse that stores {{jalink|Mikoshi|Q979940}}. === Others === * Kasagi family documents - {{jalink|Kamakura period|Q236205|Kamakura}} and {{jalink|Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|Nanbokucho}} / {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845|A group of ancient documents centered on the early Muromachi period. Contains items related to our shrine and Oyama Shrine. It was designated as a cultural property (ancient document) of Shimane Prefecture on August 12, 1975 ({{jalink|1975|Q2479}}) (part of it was added on March 28, {{jalink|Heisei|Q234510}}). === It had a close relationship with {{ill|lt=Yakehi Shrine|Q11567668|WD=Q11567668}}, which was widely worshiped as {{ill|lt=guardian deity|Q131278957|WD=Q131278957}} and was enshrined on the slope of Yakevolcano. === Enshrined God === *{{ill||lt=Mikoto Oyama|UNKNOWN}} Although I don't see any particular theories, it is said that the yakivolcano that was originally called ``Oyama'' was {{ill|lt=Shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532}} ({{ill|lt=Shintaiyama|Q1595289|WD=Q1595289}}), and that the mountain itself was worshiped {{sfn|Edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. This god and {{ill|lt=Usukega no Mikoto Shrine|Q11449116|WD=Q11449116|lt=Usukega no Mikoto}} visit {{ill|lt=Hinamajhime no Mikoto Shrine|Q133940165|WD=Q133940165|lt=Hinamajhime no Mikoto}} There is a tradition that Uukega no Mikoto won due to the interference of {{ill|lt=Amanejaki|Q2417460|WD=Q2417460}}.{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}} After that, {{ill|lt=Yakikazan|Q11567667|WD=Q11567667}} and {{ill||lt=Iekatsuyama|UNKNOWN}} (Atodo-san) competed again over {{ill||lt=Sakaki|UNKNOWN}}, and this time it was Heaven. It is said that the demons got in the way of Uukega no Mikoto, resulting in the victory of the god of Oyama, and as a result, sakaki trees flourished on Yakivolcano, but no sakaki trees grew on Mt. Ietaka {{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. It is said that the two gods are not on good terms because of this kind of relationship.{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. === History == =The date of its creation is unknown, but as mentioned above, it is believed that it was originally established as a shrine to enshrine Daisen (a volcanic volcano) as a sacred mountain. Furthermore, 200 meters west of the enshrinement site is the {{ill||lt=Hyogo Ruins|UNKNOWN}}, which appears to be a {{ill||lt=ritual ruins|UNKNOWN}} from the middle to late {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|WD=Q459939}}, and it is assumed that water or spring rituals related to agriculture were held there, but its relationship with this shrine is unknown {{sfn|Heibonsha edition|1995}}.It was known in the central government from an early period, and is listed in "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}" as a small shrine in {{ill|lt=Oki Province|Q1063829|WD=Q1063829}}{{ill|lt=Chibu District|Q11584565|WD=Q11584565}}. It is not known that the award of |Junior Third Rank |Q11591025}} is known, but in the ``{{ill|lt=Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|lt=Inshu Shinto Name Book}}'' there is no rank called ``Junior Third Rank Upper(Mama){{efn|Junior Third Rank Upper. }} Daisen Myojin” is written there. In the late {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} (around the 11th - 12th century), when {{ill|lt=Sea of Japan|Q27092|WD=Q27092}} water transport in the {{ill|lt=San'in region|Q398310|WD=Q398310}} began to develop in earnest, (Japan)||Kami}} came to be revered as the god of safe navigation, and Unjoji Temple (formerly known as Yakehi Shrine) was founded by a {{ill||lt=ascetic monk|UNKNOWN}} as a {{ill||lt=sacred place|UNKNOWN}} for {{ill||lt=mountain training|UNKNOWN}}. {{sfn|Heibonsha Edition|1995}}, and that {{ill|lt=Bettoto|Q2900274|WD=Q2900274}} came to serve as the shrine's {{ill|lt=Negi||WD=|lt=Negishiki}} {{sfn|Shikiuchisha Research Group|1983}}. (→See also "{{ill|lt=Yakehi Shrine|Q11567668|WD=Q11567668}}".)In the Middle Ages, it seems that the {{ill|lt=Izumo Genji|Q11395897|WD=Q11395897|lt=Sasaki clan}}, the {{ill|lt=Izumo Genji|Q11395897|WD=Q11395897|lt=Sasaki clan}}, who aimed for stable control of Oki Province, tried to take control of this shrine, and {{ill|lt=Kenji|Q1327993|WD=Q1327993}}On September 5, 2016 ({{ill|lt=1276|Q5530|WD=Q5530}}{{ill|lt=October 13|Q2925|WD=Q2925}}), the governor of Oki Province, {{ill|lt=Yasukiyo Sasaki|Q11382304|WD=Q11382304}}, During the Negi era, he issued a letter to Jiren, a monk whose position was in crisis due to a conflict with the shogun, and relieved the Negi position and incorporated it into the ruling party. nk|Bottom|Q11360924}}'' (Kasagi family documents){{efn|Jiren's ancestral office (Oyamasha Negi) and Kanda (Mitasho), which had been confiscated because he had violated the peasants, were returned to Jiren as a gift. }}{{sfn|Inoue|1982}}. {{Quotation|Bottom ({{ill|lt=Yasukiyo Sasaki|Q11382304|WD=Q11382304}} Kao)

Shinshi Hoso no Jyojo {{efn| (parallel translation) ``Lowered by {{ill|lt=Takaoka Muneyasu|Q6129881|WD=Q6129881}}, the guardian of Oki Province. Jiren Hoshi, a resident of Mitashō, Chio District, Oki Province (the head of Oyama Shrine whose deity is {{ill|lt=Yakizan|Q11567667|WD=Q11567667}}) is a retainer of {{ill|lt=Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881|WD=Q6129881}}. Even if there is a crime, the decision should not be made under the jurisdiction of the shogun (local government official) of Mita-sho. If any troublesome situation arises, please report the details."
April, Kenji 3 It is collected as ``Subun'', and {{harv|Inoue|1982}} attributes this to Yasukiyo's eighth son, {{ill|lt=Muneyasu Takaoka|Q6129881|WD=Q6129881}} (Hachiro). There is no dispute that ``Hachiro-dono'' in the text refers to Muneyasu, but because of the question of whether he should call himself ``Hachiro-dono,'' many say that it is a draft of ``{{ill|lt=Sasaki Yasukiyo|Q11382304|WD=Q11382304}},'' his father and the governor of Oki, but as pointed out by (Inoue 1982), the kaoki belongs to ``Muneyasu'' and is different from Yasukiyo's. This was around the time when Muneyasu had first assumed the position of shugodai, and the document was prepared by someone other than Muneyasu (probably from Jiren's side), and Muneyasu added his personal stamp to the document. It seems that there was a desire to break away from the control of the shogunate, including the parishioners of Oyama Shrine, and judging from the nature of the document, the point made by Inoue 1982 that it was a draft by Muneyasu is correct. }}''
(Kamakura Ibun Vol. 17 No. 12724)}}Furthermore, {{ill|lt=median (Era name)|Q1327153|WD=Q1327153|lt=Shochu}}3 ({{ill|lt=1326|Q5782|WD=Q5782}}), he also held the position of Shugo and was the grandson of Yasukiyo.{{ill|lt=Munekiyo Sasaki|Q221272 48|WD=Q221272 48|lt=A person presumed to be Munekiyo}} ``entrusted the monk Renjo to Kenji's {{ill|lt=shimochi|Q18226421|WD=Q18226421|lt=shimochi}}'' and assigned him to serve as a Negi-sho in social affairs and below. |Q11626805}} ``Certain Sleeve Handbook'' (Kasagi Family Documents) dated April 7, 1977{{efn|Both Jiren and Renjo have been enshrined as ``shosho handed down from generation to generation'' (jobs handed down from generation to generation). These {{ill||lt=shrine priests|UNKNOWN}} are believed to have been {{ill|lt=betto|Q2900274|WD=Q2900274}} of Unjo-ji Temple {{sfn|edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. }}. On the other hand, the shrine side (Negishiki side) has also been concerned about the current Mita area, which is centered on the enshrinement area, since it was organized into {{ill|lt=manors|Q1550557|WD=Q1550557}} and became {{ill||lt=Mita-sho|UNKNOWN}} (also called {{ill||lt=Mita-in|UNKNOWN}}), by directly connecting with the shugo power. It is said that this is where the close relationship between the two arose, with the intention of escaping from the ruling system.{{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}{{efn|In addition, Mita-sho is thought to be a manor established in the eastern half of Nishinoshima, centered around present-day Mita, Nishinoshima-cho, extending from Beppu in the east to Uragato in the west. }}. However, this relationship underwent a major change around {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho period}}, and {{ill|lt=Kenmu (Japan)|Q1193970|WD=Q1193970|lt=In the first year of Kenmu}} ({{ill|lt=1334|Q6007|WD=Q6007}}), the {{ill|lt=daikan|Q10263740|WD=Q10263740}} of Nishiryo and { Doken of {jalink|Kumon|Q11392042}}, at the behest of his feudal lord, set aside a part of Shonai as ``Oyamamiya Negibun Koushi (malt) rice farm.'' “Koshiryoba” is {{ill||lt=Divine sake|UNKNOWN}} It appears to be {{ill||lt=hatake|UNKNOWN}} for supplying {{ill|lt=koji|Q865501|WD=Q865501|lt=koji}} for brewing {{sfn|edited by Heibonsha|1995}}. }}, a situation appeared where Mitashō was being incorporated under the control, and in {{ill|lt=Oan|Q1146245|WD=Q1146245}}2 ({{ill|lt=1369|Q6211|WD=Q6211}}) The post of Negi, which became vacant after Renjo's death, was given to Doken Kumon, so ``Certain {{ill|lt=letter of fulfillment|Q11388108|WD=Q11388108}}'' (Kasagi family document) dated June 24, 2011. , it is assumed that it was completely incorporated into Mitasho, and after this point no documents related to this shrine can be seen, and the fact that related documents from before that have been handed down to the Kasagi clan, who are thought to be descendants of Doken (current Kasagi family documents), is thought to indicate this {{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}.The subsequent history is not made clear, but in the early modern period it seems to have been subsumed into Unjo-ji Temple, which was called "{{ill||lt=Yakikazan Daigongen|UNKNOWN}}", and although the Kasagi clan held the {{ill|lt=Shinto Priest|Q15888735|WD=Q15888735|lt=Shinto Priest}}, Unjo-ji Temple was in charge as a Bettoto. (Unit)|Q855048|stone}} "Oki Kokishu" of 1823, which is said to have been {{ill||lt=sharyo|UNKNOWN}}{{ill|lt=Bunsei|Q1327119|WD=Q1327119}}. is actually {{ill|lt=Chigyo|Q11584648|WD=Q11584648}} of Unjouji Temple, and it was finally realized that the ``Oyama God'' was {{ill||lt=Yakebi Gongen|UNKNOWN}} (Unjouji Temple). {{sfn|Edited by Shikinai Society Research Group|1983}}{{efn|As an example of the view of Oyama no Kami as Yakibi Gongen, {{ill|lt=Kanbun|Q1146228|WD=Q1146228}} 7th year (1667) '{ {jalink|Inshu Jōgoki|Q11659057}}'' says, ``Azuruni (Engishiki) Shinto name book, there is Oyama Shrine in Chibu district, this is the god of fire on the mountain (original Chinese)'', and ``{{jalin k|Dainihonshi|Q1156003|Dainipponshiji Jingishi}}, there is a statement that says, ``Oyama Shrine is located on the top of the Yakivolcano mountain in Hashimura, Imamida-go, and is called Yakihi Myojin (original Chinese).'' }}. In the first year of {{ill|lt=Meiji|Q3125096|WD=Q3125096}}, {{ill|lt=Separation of Shinto and Buddhism|Q1970535|WD=Q1970535}}, the shrine separated from Yakibi Gongen, and the official name of the shrine was ``Oyama Shrine'' according to the ``Engishiki'' ceremony, and in the 5th year of the Meiji era ({{ill|lt=1872|Q7753|WD=Q7753}}), it became a village shrine. === Rituals === === Shinto ritual === In "Inshu Fudoki" written in {{ill|lt=Tenpo|Q1369045|WD=Q1369045}} 4 ({{ill|lt=1833|Q7587|WD=Q7587}}), the festival dates are February 13th and June 14th, but after the implementation of {{ill|lt=solar calendar|Q210953|WD=Q210953}}, the current regular festival day is July 13th. === Priest === After the Middle Ages, the Betto-shoku of Unjo-ji Temple also served as the Negi-shoku, but in the late period, it is thought that the Kasagi family {{sfn|Edited by Heibonsha|1995}}, which seems to have taken on the Kumon-shoku of Mitashō, came to attack the Negi-shoku.In the early modern period, when Unjo-ji Temple became widely revered, Although the Kasagi family, who was in charge of the temple and served as {{ill||lt=Ōshoya|UNKNOWN}}, called themselves ``Shinto priests'', their actual involvement was limited to {{ill|lt=Danna|Q11544585|WD=Q11544585}} {{sfn|Edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. When the Meiji era policy of separating Shinto and Buddhism was carried out, in 1888, the betto of Unjo-ji Temple became {{ill|lt=returning to secular life|Q2583238|WD=Q2583238}} and became the priest (currently {{ill|lt=guji|Q11453500|WD=Q11453500}}) using the surname Matsuura, and since then the Matsuura clan has inherited the position of chief priest. === Shrine building === {{ill|lt=Main shrine|Q2044994|WD=Q2044994}} is 1 room long and has a {{ill|lt=mukōhai|Q11415970|WD=Q11415970}} of {{ill|lt=gable #gable style|Q4881511|WD=Q4881511|lt=kara gable}} on the front. It is similar to |Q3193781}}, but the facing bow is a Karahafu and its width is narrower than the width of the {{ill||lt=main building|UNKNOWN}} roof), and {{ill|lt=chigi/katsuoki|Q87220234|WD=Q87220234}} is placed on the {{ill|lt=ridge|ridge|WD=ridge|lt=Q800093}}. Rebuilt in 1899. A passage hall with 1 beam and 3 beams extends from the bottom of the worship hall, and continues with the {{ill|lt=haiden|Q3125745|WD=Q3125745}} with 5 beams and 4 beams. All have roofs {{ill||lt=copper plate|UNKNOWN}}. The {{ill|lt=torii|Q234731|WD=Q234731}} located down the stone steps in front of the shrine is {{ill|lt=granite||WD=|lt=made ​​of granite}} from {{ill|lt=Genroku|Q1325001|WD=Q1325001}}12 ({{ill|lt=1699|Q7710|WD=Q7710}}). It is the second oldest stone torii gate on Oki Island. There is also a {{ill||lt=Shinsensho|UNKNOWN}} and a Mikoshi storehouse that stores {{ill|lt=Mikoshi|Q979940|WD=Q979940}}. === Others === * Kasagi family documents - {{ill|lt=Kamakura period|Q236205|WD=Q236205|lt=Kamakura}} and {{ill|lt=Nanbokucho period (Japan)|Q264012|WD=Q264012|lt=Nanbokucho}} / {{jalink|Muromachi period|Q334845|A group of ancient documents centered on the early Muromachi period. Contains items related to our shrine and Oyama Shrine. It was designated as a cultural property (ancient document) of Shimane Prefecture on August 12, 1975 ({{ill|lt=1975|Q2479|WD=Q2479}}) (part of it was added on March 28, It had a close relationship with {{ill|lt=Yakehi Shrine|Q11567668|WD=Q11567668}}, which was widely worshiped as {{ill|lt=guardian deity|Q131278957|WD=Q131278957}} and was enshrined on the slope of Yakevolcano. === Enshrined God === *{{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Mikoto Oyama|UNKNOWN}} Although I don't see any particular theories, it is said that the yakivolcano that was originally called ``Oyama'' was {{ill|lt=Shintai|Q327532|WD=Q327532}} ({{ill|lt=Shintaiyama|Q1595289|WD=Q1595289}}), and that the mountain itself was worshiped {{sfn|Edited by Shikinaisha Research Group|1983}}. This god and {{ill|lt=Usukega no Mikoto Shrine|Q11449116|WD=Q11449116|lt=Usukega no Mikoto}} visit {{ill|lt=Hinamajhime no Mikoto Shrine|Q133940165|WD=Q133940165|lt=Hinamajhime no Mikoto}} There is a tradition that Uukega no Mikoto won due to the interference of {{ill|lt=Amanejaki|Q2417460|WD=Q2417460}}.{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}} After that, {{ill|lt=Yakikazan|Q11567667|WD=Q11567667}} and {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Iekatsuyama|UNKNOWN}} (Atodo-san) competed again over {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Sakaki|UNKNOWN}}, and this time it was Heaven. It is said that the demons got in the way of Uukega no Mikoto, resulting in the victory of the god of Oyama, and as a result, sakaki trees flourished on Yakivolcano, but no sakaki trees grew on Mt. Ietaka {{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. It is said that the two gods are not on good terms because of this kind of relationship.{{sfn|Nagami|1968|p=628}}. === History == =The date of its creation is unknown, but as mentioned above, it is believed that it was originally established as a shrine to enshrine Daisen (a volcanic volcano) as a sacred mountain. Furthermore, 200 meters west of the enshrinement site is the {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=Hyogo Ruins|UNKNOWN}}, which appears to be a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ritual ruins|UNKNOWN}} from the middle to late {{ill|lt=Kofun period|Q459939|WD=Q459939}}, and it is assumed that water or spring rituals related to agriculture were held there, but its relationship with this shrine is unknown {{sfn|Heibonsha edition|1995}}.It was known in the central government from an early period, and is listed in "{{ill|lt=Engishiki Shinmyocho|Q11064932|WD=Q11064932}}" as a small shrine in {{ill|lt=Oki Province|Q1063829|WD=Q1063829}}{{ill|lt=Chibu District|Q11584565|WD=Q11584565}}. It is not known that the award of |Junior Third Rank |Q11591025}} is known, but in the ``{{ill|lt=Domestic Shinto Name Book|Q11420490|WD=Q11420490|lt=Inshu Shinto Name Book}}'' there is no rank called ``Junior Third Rank Upper(Mama){{efn|Junior Third Rank Upper. }} Daisen Myojin” is written there. In the late {{ill|lt=Heian period|Q193292|WD=Q193292}} (around the 11th - 12th century), when {{ill|lt=Sea of Japan|Q27092|WD=Q27092}} water transport in the {{ill|lt=San'in region|Q398310|WD=Q398310}} began to develop in earnest, (Japan)||Kami}} came to be revered as the god of safe navigation, and Unjoji Temple (formerly known as Yakehi Shrine) was founded by a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=ascetic monk|UNKNOWN}} as a {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=sacred place|UNKNOWN}} for {{ill|UNKNOWN|lt=mountain training|UNKNOWN}}. {{sfn|Heibonsha Edition|1995}}, and that {{ill|lt=Bettoto|Q2900274|WD=Q2900274}} came to serve as the shrine's {{ill|lt=Negi||WD=|lt=Negishiki}} {{sfn|Shikiuchisha Research Group|1983}}. (→See also "{{ill|lt=Yakehi Shrine|Q11567668|WD=Q11567668}}".)In the Middle Ages, it seems that the {{ill|lt=Izumo Genji|Q11395897|WD=Q11395897|lt=Sasaki clan}}, the {{ill|lt=Izumo Genji|Q11395897|WD=Q11395897|lt=Sasaki clan}}, who aimed for stable control of Oki Province, tried to take control of this shrine, and {{ill|lt=Kenji|Q1327993|WD=Q1327993}}On September 5, 2016 ({{ill|lt=1276|Q5530|WD=Q5530}}{{ill|lt=October 13|Q2925|WD=Q2925}}), the governor of Oki Province, {{ill|lt=Yasukiyo Sasaki|Q11382304|WD=Q11382304}}, During the Negi era, he issued a letter to Jiren, a monk whose position was in crisis due to a conflict with the shogun, and relieved the Negi position and incorporated it into the ruling party. nk|Bottom|Q11360924}}'' (Kasagi family documents){{efn|Jiren's ancestral office (Oyamasha Negi) and Kanda (Mitasho), which had been confiscated because he had violated the peasants, were returned to Jiren as a gift. }}{{sfn|Inoue|1982}}. {{Quotation|Bottom ({{ill|lt=Yasukiyo Sasaki|Q11382304|WD=Q11382304}} Kao)
